html:
Greta Lovisa Garbo, originally Gustafsson, was born on September 18, 1905, in the Katarina parish of Stockholm, and died on April 15, 1990, in New York, USA. She was a Swedish-American actress who became one of the biggest movie stars of the 1920s and 1930s. The American Film Institute ranked Garbo as the fifth greatest female Hollywood actor of all time.

Greta Garbo was also known for her shyness towards the public, which persisted until her death. After her career took off, she never gave any interviews or signed autographs. She never responded to fan mail, and she never attended any film premieres.

== Biography ==

=== Childhood === Garbo was the daughter of Karl Alfred Gustafsson, a sanitation worker, and his wife, Anna Lovisa Johansson. The parents moved from Småland to Stockholm at the turn of the century (1900). She had two older siblings: Sven and Alva. Her sister died at the age of 22. Her father died of Spanish flu when Greta was 14 years old. Her parents and sister Alva are buried in the Skogskyrkogården cemetery, plot 19-00056. Her brother emigrated to America, where he died in 1967. During Garbo's childhood, the family lived in a one-room apartment at Blekingegatan 32 in Stockholm. Her mother sometimes worked extra as a seamstress. She eventually also emigrated to America and died there in 1944. Greta attended Katarina södra elementary school in Södermalm and graduated at the age of 14 in 1919 with good grades. She was confirmed in 1920 at Katarina Church.

=== Early Film Roles ===

When Greta Garbo was 14 years old, she got a job at Ekengren's hair salon on Götgatan in Södermalm, Stockholm. Kristian Bergström was a customer there and offered her a job at the department store PUB, which was owned by his father, Paul U. Bergström. She started as a sales assistant in the hat department at PUB in July 1920, and eventually also worked as a model for various advertisements. Her first film role was in a short advertising film for the department store PUB called "Gentlemen Shopping in Stockholm" (premiere December 12, 1920). She had her first role in a feature film, a small part as a maid, in the movie "A Knight of Fortune" (1921). She got a slightly larger supporting role in the comedy "The Tramp Petter" (1922). In order to participate in the film and later enroll in the Dramaten acting school, she quit her job at PUB on July 22, 1922.

=== Dramaten Acting School and Garbo's Discovery ===

From 1922 to 1924, Garbo studied at the Royal Dramatic Theatre School in Stockholm, where her classmates included Mimi Pollak, Vera Schmiterlöw, and the future director Alf Sjöberg. Garbo made her debut at the Dramaten in April 1923 as Mrs. von Brandt in Richard Kessler's play "Sköldpaddskammen." During her two years at the national theatre, she performed in productions alongside renowned actors of the 19th century such as Nils Personne, August Lindberg, and Julia Håkansson, as well as prominent actors of the early 20th century like Harriet Bosse, Jessie Wessel, Anders de Wahl, Maria Schildknecht, Ivar Kåge, Gabriel Alw, and Märta Ekström. Her last role at the Dramaten was Mariette in Jules Romains' "Knock" in late 1924.

In the autumn of 1923, Garbo was hired as a model for NK's French women's clothing department. She modeled garments by Premet, Renée, and Drecoll, receiving a fee of 50 krona.

Around midsummer in 1924, she met Ivar Kreuger during a visit to Stockholm by Mary Pickford and Douglas Fairbanks, organized by Svensk Filmindustri and Charles Magnusson. Kreuger invited the entire group on a boat trip in his yacht, Loris. Mauritz Stiller, the film director, is considered the one who "discovered" Garbo. He had been tipped off about her by his colleague Gustaf Molander, who was the head of the Dramaten School at the time. Stiller gave her a role in his film "Gösta Berlings saga" (1924).

The screenwriter Arthur Nordén is said to be the one who suggested that Greta Gustafsson change her name to Garbo, during a planned film production with Stiller where she was supposed to have the leading role. "Garbo" is a distortion of the name of King Gustav II Adolf's brother-in-law, Gábor Bethlen, a name that Nordén often spoke about in his history classes at the folk high school Hvilan from 1915 to 1918, before he became a screenwriter for Stiller. On December 4, 1923, the Stockholm City Police Board approved the name change from Greta Gustafsson to Greta Garbo, citing it as a family name.

"Gösta Berlings saga" became a commercial success in Germany, which led to Stiller and Garbo receiving several offers to make films there. Together, they worked on a film project called "Die Odaliske von Smolny," but it was never completed. The following year, Greta Garbo received one of the leading roles in Georg Wilhelm Pabst's acclaimed silent film, "The Joyless Street" (Die freudlose Gasse) (1925).

=== To America ===

In 1925, Mauritz Stiller and Greta Garbo were invited to Hollywood by Louis B. Mayer, the founder of the film studio Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer (MGM). Louis Mayer was impressed by both Stiller's work as a director, but especially by Garbo's performance in the silent film "Gösta Berlings saga," which he had seen in Berlin. As Garbo became more famous...

How certain are we about this?

People from the 20th century are extremely well-documented. Uncertainty rarely concerns facts themselves, but rather interpretations and evaluations, which can change over time and depending on the observer.
tldr: Greta Garbo was one of the most famous movie stars in the world during the 1920s and 30s. She became renowned for her acting, but she chose to maintain a very private life and never gave any interviews.
lattlast:
Greta Garbo was born in Stockholm in 1905 and started working at a young age, first as a shop assistant and then as a model. She later studied acting at the Dramaten school, which provided training for aspiring actors. It was there that she was discovered by director Mauritz Stiller, who gave her a role in the film "Gösta Berlings saga."

When she became famous, she changed her last name from Gustafsson to Garbo. She achieved great success in Europe and later moved to the United States to work in Hollywood. There, she became one of the most important actresses of all time, according to experts in film history.

Despite her immense fame, Greta Garbo was very private. She didn't want to be the center of attention and never responded to letters from fans or gave interviews to newspapers. She chose to keep her personal life completely separate from her career as a star.
